content.heading: Data Types — what kind of thing is this?

blocks.text: Every value in JavaScript has a type. The type determines what you can do with it. You can't do math with text, and you can't use string methods on a number.

blocks.label: Remember!
blocks.text: Knowing the types helps you avoid bugs that seem totally random but have a very simple cause.
You can check the type of a value using the `typeof` operator.

blocks.desc: The typeof operator tells you what the type is for 'variableName'. It returns a string of the name of the type: 'number', 'string', or 'boolean'.

blocks.label: The most common beginner trap
blocks.text: If a number comes from a text input, it might arrive as a 'string'. '100' + 1 is '1001', not 101!

exercises.title: What type is this?
tasks: What is the data type of this value: '100'?
exercises.hint: Look closely. Does this value have quotes around it?
exercises.explanation: '100' has quotes, which makes it a string—not a number. In Simon Says, if a score comes from a text input, it often arrives as a string and needs conversion.

exercises.title: What does typeof return?
exercises.hint: typeof always returns a string—the name of the type, in lowercase.
exercises.explanation: The `typeof` operator always returns a string naming the type. Since `true` is a boolean, it returns 'boolean'. This helps us verify our game logic variables are valid booleans.

exercises.title: Sort by type
tasks: Drag each value into the correct type bucket.
exercises.hint: Quotes always make a string. true and false without quotes are booleans.
exercises.explanation: 'false' in quotes is a string, not a boolean. This is a common trap when receiving data from users in game menus.

exercises.title: Drop the typeof keyword
tasks: Drop the correct keyword to check the type of each variable.
exercises.hint: The operator that checks types is one word, all lowercase.
exercises.explanation: The operator is `typeof`. Using 'type' or 'getType' would cause a ReferenceError in your game code.

exercises.title: Pick the right typed value
tasks: Drop the correct value for each variable so it has the right type: score (number), playerName (string), isGameOver (boolean).
exercises.hint: Numbers have no quotes. Strings have quotes. Booleans are true or false with no quotes.
exercises.explanation: If `isGameOver` was `'false'` (a string), the game logic might treat it as 'truthy' and end the game early. Always use the literal boolean values.

exercises.title: Log the types in order
tasks: Put these lines in order so variables are declared before their types are logged.
exercises.hint: A variable must exist before you can check what it is.
exercises.explanation: In JavaScript, order matters. We declare our Simon Says state variables first, then we can use `typeof` to inspect them.

exercises.title: Type the typeof expressions
tasks: Type the variable name inside each typeof call.
exercises.hint: The variable name goes directly after the typeof keyword.
exercises.explanation: By typing the variable names, you're telling the program which piece of Simon Says data you want to inspect.

exercises.title: Does 'true' equal true?
exercises.hint: === checks both the value AND the type.
exercises.explanation: A string `'true'` is not the same as the boolean `true`. Using the wrong type is the most common reason Simon Says levels don't end when they should.

exercises.title: Correct the assignments
tasks: Assign the correct literals so score is a number and isGameOver is a boolean.
exercises.explanation: Writing literals without quotes ensures the computer treats them as the correct data type for game math and logic.

exercises.title: Find the type bug
tasks: Use the typeof output to find the variable with the wrong type.
Fix it so score is a number. Hit Run.

steps.text: Run the code to see that score is currently a string.
Remove the quotes from '120' on line 1 so it becomes a number.

exercises.explanation: Removing quotes is the simplest way to fix 'Type Errors' in your Simon Says score tracking.

exercises.title: Fix the broken addition
tasks: Convert the string input to a number so the total is 60.
exercises.hint: Use Number(playerInput) to convert the type.

steps.text: Run the code. Notice it logs '5010' because it's adding a string to a number.
Update line 3 to: let total = Number(playerInput) + bonus;

exercises.explanation: The `Number()` function is your 'escape hatch' when user input arrives as a string but needs to be added to a game score.

exercises.title: Build the type report
tasks: Log the variable and its type using a template literal.

steps.text: Now do the same for the score variable. Hit Run.

exercises.explanation: This report style is a professional way to debug your Simon Says game state when things act weird.

challenges.title: Mystery Types
tasks: Run the code to see what JavaScript thinks of these special cases.
Fix the code so isGameOver is a real boolean.
challenges.hint: === checks both the value AND the type. 'true' and true look the same when logged, but typeof will reveal the difference.
challenges.explanation: isGameOver is set to 'true' in quotes, making it a string, not a boolean. The condition isGameOver === true fails because 'true' (string) is not the same as true (boolean).
